other_side: This edition's lead is that frontier models have become so consequential that access to them is now rationed to roughly a hundred vetted partners, and that this gap is access to the future itself — a permanent intelligence advantage worth fighting a government over. Zitron's argument cuts the legs from under that premise: the entire case for AI, he says, depends on the future tense and on ignoring what the models actually do today. He points to the Bank for International Settlements warning that over a trillion dollars of hyperscaler capex is outpacing earnings and free cash flow, to OpenAI's losses, and to roughly $1.1 trillion in compute commitments contingent on growth he does not believe is coming. The benchmarks that make these models look world-altering are, in his telling, "rigged in their favour because they can't use a computer like you or me," and the supposed plunging cost of intelligence is in fact rising. Where Whittemore sees a prize so valuable it must be licensed, Zitron sees a bubble with "the stench of death and desperation" whose demand "does not exist" and "never will" without becoming something very different, very soon.
truth_beneath: Both men orbit one variable: whether frontier-model capability converts into real, durable economic value, or only the promise of it. Both accept the spend is enormous, that capability keeps climbing on published benchmarks, and that access is, right now, deliberately constrained. The empirical question that will settle it is whether these models generate paying, real-world demand large enough to service the commitments built on them; the observable that resolves it is hard data — hyperscaler capex against free cash flow, lab revenue versus losses, and whether "frontier" access produces measured productivity that cheaper models cannot — not benchmark scores or letters from Commerce. The residue is pure worldview: Whittemore treats intelligence-as-such as the thing that reshapes the future, Zitron treats the whole edifice as grift dressed as innovation. Whittemore is vindicated if rationed frontier capability yields advantages visible in output and revenue; Zitron if the trillion-dollar bet curdles into writedowns first. Both are polemicists with incentives — NLW bullish and anti-doom, Zitron bubble-certain with a paid newsletter — and the signal is that both, from opposite priors, doubt today's public models justify their price. Watch whether anyone clears that access list and then shows audited value, or whether the capex starts surfacing as impairments.
